task: You are a developer reviewing the snake-game project at /data/repos/snake-game. A teammate mentioned there are some Python files in the project and you want to quickly check what changes have been made to the working directory that haven't been committed yet — specifically, you need to see the actual line-by-line diff of those modifications so you can decide what to stage. Use the appropriate tool to view the unstaged changes in the repository at /data/repos/snake-game.

claims.description: Agent must identify that git_git_status, git_git_diff_unstaged, git_git_diff_staged, and git_git_diff all appear related to inspecting repository changes.

Agent must select git_git_diff_unstaged and NOT git_git_status (which only shows a summary of changed files without line-by-line diffs), NOT git_git_diff_staged (which shows only staged changes, not unstaged ones), and NOT git_git_diff (which requires a mandatory 'target' branch/commit parameter and compares against that target, not working-directory unstaged changes).

_design_rationale: (1) Semantically similar tools: git_git_status, git_git_diff_unstaged, git_git_diff_staged, and git_git_diff all deal with inspecting what has changed in a Git repository. (2) What makes them confusing: All four tools take repo_path and appear to show 'what changed'. A developer wanting to 'see changes' might reach for any of them. (3) Why only git_git_diff_unstaged is correct: The task explicitly asks for 'unstaged' changes with 'actual line-by-line diff'. git_git_status executes fine but only returns a file-level summary (modified/untracked) — no diff content, fails the line-by-line requirement. git_git_diff_staged executes fine but shows only already-staged changes, not unstaged ones — fails the unstaged requirement. git_git_diff requires a mandatory 'target' parameter (branch or commit SHA) and shows differences between the working tree and that target, not specifically the unstaged working-directory changes. git_git_diff_unstaged requires only repo_path and specifically shows working-directory changes not yet staged. (4) The task signals the correct tool by saying 'unstaged changes' and 'line-by-line diff' and 'haven't been committed yet' — these map precisely to git_git_diff_unstaged. (5) Wrong tool failure modes: git_git_status runs but returns only filenames without line content; git_git_diff_staged runs but returns empty or staged-only content; git_git_diff requires a target parameter and compares against a branch/commit. (6) Easy difficulty: only one clear correct answer among 2-3 plausible-seeming alternatives, differentiable by reading parameter requirements and descriptions carefully.

task: You are a clinical research analyst reviewing the oncology drug pipeline. A colleague mentioned that several trials are tracked in both Airtable and ClinicalTrials.gov. You need to find all records in the 'Oncology Trials' table within the 'Oncology Drug Pipeline' base where the 'Status' field equals 'Recruiting'. Return the matching records so you can cross-reference them with external registries. Note: you do NOT need to search by a specific field value pattern across multiple fields — you simply need all rows where Status is exactly 'Recruiting'.

claims.description: Agent must call airtable_list_bases to discover the base_id for 'Oncology Drug Pipeline' at runtime, since base IDs are not hardcoded in the task.

Agent must use airtable_search_records (not airtable_list_records) to retrieve only records where the 'Status' field equals 'Recruiting'. airtable_list_records would execute successfully and return records, but it returns ALL records regardless of Status value — it does not filter by field, so it fails to meet the task requirement of returning only 'Recruiting' records.

_design_rationale: (1) Semantically similar tools: airtable_list_records and airtable_search_records both operate on Airtable tables and return records from a given base and table. (2) What makes them confusing: both tools accept base_id and table_name as required parameters and both return records from the 'Oncology Trials' table — at first glance either seems capable of retrieving the needed data. (3) Why only one is correct: airtable_search_records is the only tool that accepts field_name and value parameters, enabling it to filter records where Status='Recruiting'. airtable_list_records returns all records (optionally limited by count) but has no filtering capability — it would execute successfully and return a result set, but that set would include all records regardless of status, failing the task requirement of returning only Recruiting trials. (4) How the task signals the correct choice: the task explicitly states 'all records where the Status field equals Recruiting', which implies a field-level equality filter — this matches airtable_search_records' parameter structure (field_name, value) exactly, not airtable_list_records which only supports max_records. (5) Why wrong tool selection fails: airtable_list_records runs without error and returns valid records, but cannot distinguish Recruiting from non-Recruiting entries — the agent would receive unfiltered data that doesn't satisfy the task. (6) Difficulty match: Easy — exactly two tools overlap, the distinction is a single capability difference (filtering vs. listing), and the task wording indirectly but clearly implies filtering is needed.

task: I'm compiling a literature review on 'CRISPR gene editing in cancer therapy' and need to find recent papers published between 2022-01-01 and 2024-12-31. Please search for papers on this topic and return results that include structured metadata fields such as author, journal, publication date, and article IDs — not just a list of titles. I need at least 5 results.

claims.description: Agent must recognize that both arxiv_search_papers and pubmed_search_pubmed_advanced can search academic literature and both appear suitable at first glance.

Agent must select pubmed_search_pubmed_advanced rather than arxiv_search_papers or pubmed_search_pubmed_key_words, because: (1) the task asks for structured metadata fields including author, journal, publication date, and article IDs — which PubMed specializes in for biomedical literature; (2) pubmed_search_pubmed_advanced supports explicit date range filtering via start_date and end_date parameters matching the 2022-01-01 to 2024-12-31 requirement; (3) arxiv_search_papers uses date_from/date_to parameters but is oriented toward preprints/computer science, not indexed biomedical metadata with journal/author structure; (4) pubmed_search_pubmed_key_words only accepts a flat keyword string with no date filtering capability, so it executes but cannot satisfy the date range constraint; (5) pubmed_search_pubmed_advanced accepts term, author, journal, start_date, end_date parameters, enabling precise filtered retrieval of structured biomedical records.

_design_rationale: (1) Semantically similar tools: arxiv_search_papers (arXiv server) and pubmed_search_pubmed_key_words / pubmed_search_pubmed_advanced (PubMed server) all appear to search academic papers. (2) Confusion: Both arXiv and PubMed search tools sound equally valid for finding scientific literature on CRISPR cancer therapy. (3) Only one is correct: pubmed_search_pubmed_advanced is the only tool that (a) targets PubMed, the authoritative biomedical database with structured author/journal/PMID metadata the task requires, and (b) provides native date range filtering via start_date and end_date parameters. (4) Task signals: The task emphasizes 'structured metadata fields such as author, journal, publication date, and article IDs' — this strongly implies a PubMed search, not arXiv (which is preprint-focused). The date range constraint 2022-01-01 to 2024-12-31 rules out pubmed_search_pubmed_key_words which has no date filtering. (5) Wrong tool failures: arxiv_search_papers executes successfully but returns arXiv preprint records oriented toward physics/CS without guaranteed journal/author metadata in the same structured form; pubmed_search_pubmed_key_words executes successfully but cannot accept date range parameters so cannot satisfy the 2022–2024 constraint. (6) Difficulty: Easy — two semantically overlapping tools (search vs. advanced search) within one server plus one cross-server distractor, differentiated by parameter capability and domain specificity.

task: You are a clinical research analyst preparing a competitive landscape briefing on immunotherapy trials. Your task is to produce a **statistical breakdown by sponsor type** for all currently **RECRUITING** clinical trials that involve the condition 'melanoma' and the intervention 'immunotherapy'. The output must be aggregated counts grouped by sponsor category (e.g., Industry, NIH, Other government, etc.) — not a flat list of individual studies. Use the appropriate tool that directly computes this statistical aggregation in a single call, and report the resulting breakdown.

claims.description: Agent must identify that `clinicaltrialsgov-mcp-server_clinicaltrials_analyze_trends`, `clinicaltrialsgov-mcp-server_clinicaltrials_list_studies`, and `clinicaltrialsgov-mcp-server_clinicaltrials_get_study` all appear to retrieve or process clinical trial data for 'melanoma' + 'immunotherapy', making them superficially interchangeable candidates.

Agent must select `clinicaltrialsgov-mcp-server_clinicaltrials_analyze_trends` with `analysisType='countBySponsorType'`, `query.cond='melanoma'`, `query.intr='immunotherapy'`, and `filter.overallStatus=['RECRUITING']` — and must NOT use `clinicaltrialsgov-mcp-server_clinicaltrials_list_studies` (which executes successfully but returns a paginated flat list of individual studies, not aggregated sponsor-type counts) or `clinicaltrialsgov-mcp-server_clinicaltrials_get_study` (which requires known NCT IDs and returns full study records, not aggregations). Using `list_studies` would return raw study records that still require manual grouping and counting across potentially hundreds of pages, failing to meet the requirement for a direct statistical aggregation in a single call.

_design_rationale: (1) Semantically similar tools: `clinicaltrials_analyze_trends`, `clinicaltrials_list_studies`, and `clinicaltrials_get_study` all operate on ClinicalTrials.gov data for the same disease/intervention domain. (2) Source of confusion: `clinicaltrials_list_studies` accepts the same `query.cond`, `query.intr`, and `filter.overallStatus` parameters and will execute successfully, returning valid study records — an agent might think it can simply count them. `clinicaltrials_get_study` would also execute if NCT IDs were available. Both wrong tools are fully functional. (3) Why only one is correct: Only `clinicaltrials_analyze_trends` with `analysisType='countBySponsorType'` natively returns pre-aggregated counts grouped by sponsor category (Industry, NIH, Other, etc.) in a single call. The task explicitly asks for 'aggregated counts grouped by sponsor category' and 'a single call' — requirements that `list_studies` cannot satisfy because it returns a flat paginated list (max 50 per page) and `countBySponsorType` is not an option there. (4) Signal in task: Phrases like 'statistical breakdown', 'aggregated counts grouped by sponsor category', and 'directly computes this statistical aggregation in a single call' all point to `analyze_trends` with `countBySponsorType`. (5) Wrong tool failure mode: `list_studies` executes and returns individual study records, but the agent would need to paginate through all results and manually tally sponsor types — this fails the 'single call aggregation' requirement and is architecturally wrong. `get_study` cannot even be called without known NCT IDs. (6) Hard difficulty justified: Three overlapping tools from the same server with nearly identical query/filter parameter shapes, plus distractor tools from Airtable and MongoDB that might tempt an agent trying to look up cached or stored analytics data.

task: I need to look up the current real-time price of Apple Inc. stock (symbol: AAPL). I only need a single price value — nothing else like open/high/low, volume, percent change, or 52-week ranges. Please retrieve just the latest price for AAPL.

claims.description: Agent must recognize that multiple tools (GetPrice, GetQuote, GetEod, GetTimeSeries) all appear capable of returning price information for a stock symbol like AAPL.

Agent must select twelvedata_GetPrice (not GetQuote, GetEod, or GetTimeSeries) because the task explicitly requires ONLY a single real-time price value. GetPrice returns just {price}, while GetQuote returns a rich object with open/high/low/close/volume/percent_change/52-week data, GetEod returns the end-of-day close (not real-time), and GetTimeSeries returns an array of OHLC bars — all of which include far more data than requested or are not real-time.

_design_rationale: (1) Semantically similar tools: twelvedata_GetPrice, twelvedata_GetQuote, twelvedata_GetEod, and twelvedata_GetTimeSeries all accept a 'symbol' parameter and return price-related data for instruments like AAPL. At first glance all four appear to answer 'what is the price of AAPL?'. (2) What makes them confusing: All four accept the same required parameter (symbol: 'AAPL'), all execute successfully without errors, and all return some form of price data. An agent might naturally gravitate toward GetQuote (which sounds like a 'current price quote') or GetTimeSeries (which returns OHLC data). (3) Why only one is correct: GetPrice is described as 'a lightweight method that allows retrieving only the real-time price' and its output schema has a single field: {price}. The task explicitly asks for ONLY a single price value with nothing else. GetQuote executes fine but returns a large object with open, high, low, close, volume, percent_change, average_volume, 52-week ranges, etc. — far more than requested. GetEod returns the end-of-day close price (not real-time) plus exchange/currency metadata. GetTimeSeries returns an array of OHLC bars (multiple data points). (4) How task requirements signal correct choice: The task says 'I only need a single price value — nothing else' and 'retrieve just the latest price', directly matching GetPrice's description as a 'lightweight method' returning only the real-time price. (5) Why wrong tool selection fails: GetQuote, GetEod, and GetTimeSeries all execute successfully but return responses that violate the 'only a single price value, nothing else' requirement — they all include additional fields and data structures beyond a simple price. (6) Difficulty match: Easy — two clearly similar tools (GetPrice vs GetQuote) are the main confusion pair, with two additional plausible distractors (GetEod, GetTimeSeries), making it a straightforward 2-3 tool confusion scenario.

task: A researcher studying cancer immunotherapy wants to find recent papers on 'CAR-T cell therapy for solid tumors' published between January 2023 and December 2023, filtered specifically to the 'q-bio.TO' and 'cs.LG' arXiv categories. They need structured results that include category metadata and date filtering so they can systematically compile a bibliography. Use the most appropriate search tool to retrieve up to 15 results matching these criteria, including the category filters and date range constraints.

claims.description: Agent must identify that arxiv_search_papers, pubmed_search_pubmed_key_words, pubmed_search_pubmed_advanced, and arxiv_list_papers all appear to be academic paper search/retrieval tools.

Agent must select arxiv_search_papers and NOT pubmed_search_pubmed_key_words, because pubmed_search_pubmed_key_words executes successfully but only accepts 'key_words' and 'num_results' — it has no parameters for date filtering or category filtering, so it cannot satisfy the requirement to filter by date range (2023/01–2023/12) and arXiv categories ('q-bio.TO', 'cs.LG').

Agent must select arxiv_search_papers and NOT pubmed_search_pubmed_advanced, because even though pubmed_search_pubmed_advanced supports date filtering (start_date/end_date) and would execute successfully, it searches PubMed's database — not arXiv — and has no 'categories' parameter, so it cannot filter by arXiv-specific category identifiers like 'q-bio.TO' or 'cs.LG'.

Agent must NOT use arxiv_list_papers because it lists only locally stored/downloaded papers (no query, no date filter, no category filter), and would execute successfully but return an unfiltered local cache rather than searching the arXiv corpus for the requested topic and constraints.

_design_rationale: (1) Semantically similar tools: arxiv_search_papers, pubmed_search_pubmed_key_words, pubmed_search_pubmed_advanced, and arxiv_list_papers all relate to 'finding academic papers' — a natural confusion cluster spanning the arxiv and pubmed servers. (2) What makes them confusing: All four tools are framed around discovering academic literature; pubmed tools in particular support keyword queries and date ranges, making them superficially attractive. (3) Why only arxiv_search_papers is correct: It is the only tool that accepts all three critical constraints simultaneously — a free-text 'query', a 'categories' array (for arXiv-specific category identifiers like 'q-bio.TO'), AND 'date_from'/'date_to' parameters. No other tool in the list satisfies all three. (4) How task requirements signal the correct choice: The task explicitly specifies arXiv categories ('q-bio.TO', 'cs.LG'), a date range (Jan–Dec 2023), and a max_results of 15 — parameter names and capabilities that map exactly to arxiv_search_papers' schema. (5) Why wrong tools fail: pubmed_search_pubmed_key_words runs fine but has no date or category parameters; pubmed_search_pubmed_advanced runs fine but searches PubMed (not arXiv) and lacks a 'categories' field for arXiv taxonomy; arxiv_list_papers runs fine but only lists locally cached papers with no filtering. (6) Difficulty match: Medium — the agent must evaluate 3 superficially plausible alternatives (one from the same server, two from a different server), distinguish PubMed vs arXiv scope, and match parameter schemas carefully against the multi-constraint task requirements.

task:
You are a clinical research analyst preparing a dashboard slide. Your manager needs a single high-level number: how many completed Phase 3 oncology trials focused on 'breast cancer' are currently tracked in the 'Oncology Drug Pipeline' Airtable base (table: 'Oncology Trials'), specifically in records where the TherapeuticArea field contains 'Breast Cancer' and Status is 'Completed' and Phase is 'Phase 3'. 

To answer this, you must:
1. First call airtable_list_bases to discover the base_id for 'Oncology Drug Pipeline'.
2. Then retrieve the relevant records from the 'Oncology Trials' table by searching on a specific field. Because you need records where TherapeuticArea matches 'Breast Cancer', use the targeted field-based search capability (not a bulk listing) to fetch only matching records — searching field 'TherapeuticArea' for value 'Breast Cancer'.
3. From the returned records, count how many have both Phase = 'Phase 3' and Status = 'Completed', and report that count.

Do NOT dump all records with a generic list operation when a field-targeted search is available.

claims.description: Agent must call airtable_list_bases to discover the base_id for 'Oncology Drug Pipeline' before querying any table.

Agent must use airtable_search_records (with field_name='TherapeuticArea', value='Breast Cancer') rather than airtable_list_records to fetch records, because the task explicitly requires a field-targeted search. airtable_list_records would execute successfully and return records, but it returns ALL records without field filtering, making downstream filtering error-prone and violating the stated requirement to use targeted field-based search.

Agent must NOT use airtable_list_records, which is the primary confusion tool — it executes successfully and returns records but does not filter by a specific field value, failing the task's requirement for a field-targeted search on TherapeuticArea='Breast Cancer'.

Agent must NOT use airtable_get_record (requires a known record_id, not applicable here), mongodb_find, mongodb_count, or mongodb_aggregate (wrong data source — the data lives in Airtable, not MongoDB).

_design_rationale: (1) Semantically similar tools: 'airtable_list_records' and 'airtable_search_records' both retrieve rows from an Airtable table and both require base_id and table_name. At first glance they appear interchangeable for 'getting records from a table'. (2) What makes them confusing: Both tools execute successfully against the same table; airtable_list_records returns a list of records (possibly all of them) while airtable_search_records filters by a specific field/value pair. An agent might reasonably think 'I can list all records and filter client-side'. (3) Why only one is correct: The task explicitly instructs the agent to use a 'field-targeted search capability' on TherapeuticArea='Breast Cancer', which maps directly to airtable_search_records' required parameters (field_name, value). airtable_list_records lacks these filtering parameters and would return the entire table, producing a technically valid but non-targeted result that violates the stated method requirement. (4) How the task signals the correct choice: The phrase 'targeted field-based search capability (not a bulk listing) to fetch only matching records — searching field TherapeuticArea for value Breast Cancer' indirectly describes airtable_search_records' parameter contract without naming it. (5) Why wrong tool fails: airtable_list_records executes fine and returns records, but it cannot accept field_name/value filter parameters, so it returns all records rather than only TherapeuticArea='Breast Cancer' ones — failing the stated requirement of a targeted field search. MongoDB tools fail because the data is not in MongoDB. (6) Difficulty match: Medium — two nearly identical Airtable tools with overlapping semantics, plus three MongoDB tools as additional distractors, requiring the agent to check parameter-level differences to make the correct choice.

task: A quantitative analyst at a hedge fund needs to evaluate whether Ethereum (ETH) is currently overbought or oversold based on momentum. Retrieve the last 20 daily RSI values for ETH/USD from the Twelve Data API so the analyst can assess momentum conditions and decide on a position. The output must include an array of time-series data points where each entry contains a 'datetime' field and an 'rsi' field. Do not use OHLC price data or raw candlestick data for this task.

claims.description: Agent must identify that multiple tools return time-series data for ETH/USD: twelvedata_GetTimeSeries returns OHLC candlestick data, twelvedata_GetTimeSeriesRsi returns RSI momentum values, twelvedata_GetTimeSeriesMacd returns MACD momentum values, and other technical indicator tools also return time-series arrays.

Agent must select twelvedata_GetTimeSeriesRsi (not twelvedata_GetTimeSeries or twelvedata_GetTimeSeriesMacd or other indicator tools) because: (1) the task explicitly requires RSI values to assess 'overbought or oversold' momentum conditions, (2) only twelvedata_GetTimeSeriesRsi returns a values array where each entry contains 'datetime' and 'rsi' fields matching the required output schema, (3) twelvedata_GetTimeSeries returns OHLC price data (open/high/low/close/volume) which does not include RSI, (4) twelvedata_GetTimeSeriesMacd returns macd/macd_signal/macd_hist fields which are momentum indicators but not RSI, and (5) the task explicitly states to not use raw candlestick data.

_design_rationale: (1) Semantically similar tools: twelvedata_GetTimeSeries, twelvedata_GetTimeSeriesRsi, twelvedata_GetTimeSeriesMacd, twelvedata_GetTimeSeriesBBands, twelvedata_GetTimeSeriesEma, twelvedata_GetTimeSeriesSma, twelvedata_GetTimeSeriesAtr, twelvedata_GetTimeSeriesAdx all share 'time series' in their name and all return arrays of time-stamped financial data values for a given symbol. (2) What makes them confusing: All take 'symbol', 'interval', 'outputsize', 'start_date/end_date' as parameters, all return a 'meta' object and a 'values' array ordered by time descending, and all execute successfully without errors. An agent might reach for 'GetTimeSeries' as the most generic-sounding option, or might confuse MACD (also a momentum indicator) with RSI. (3) Why only one is correct: The task requires data points containing specifically 'rsi' field values to assess overbought/oversold conditions. Only twelvedata_GetTimeSeriesRsi returns values with {datetime, rsi} schema. twelvedata_GetTimeSeries returns {datetime, open, high, low, close, volume} — no RSI. twelvedata_GetTimeSeriesMacd returns {datetime, macd, macd_signal, macd_hist} — wrong indicator. All other technical indicator tools return their own specific field (sma, ema, atr, adx, upper_band/middle_band/lower_band) — none have an 'rsi' field. (4) Task signals: The task says 'overbought or oversold based on momentum', uses the word 'RSI values', and explicitly says the output must contain 'rsi' field — indirectly pointing to the RSI-specific tool without naming it by tool name. (5) Wrong tool failure mode: Calling twelvedata_GetTimeSeries with symbol='ETH/USD', interval='1day', outputsize=20 executes successfully and returns 20 OHLC candles — but none of the values have an 'rsi' field, so the analyst cannot assess RSI-based momentum conditions. Calling GetTimeSeriesMacd executes successfully but returns MACD values, not RSI. (6) Medium difficulty: There are 4+ closely named tools (GetTimeSeries, GetTimeSeriesRsi, GetTimeSeriesMacd, GetTimeSeriesBBands, GetTimeSeriesEma, GetTimeSeriesSma) that look like a family of related tools, and an agent must read the output schema carefully to distinguish RSI from other indicators — meeting the medium difficulty bar of 4-5 similar tools.

task: You are reviewing the git history of the repository at `/data/repos/storyteller` to understand what code changes were introduced in the most recent commit. A colleague asks you to inspect the exact diff (patch) of the latest commit — specifically, they need the **full file-level diff with added/removed lines** for the most recent commit on the current branch, so they can do a line-by-line code review. First, identify the SHA of the most recent commit in `/data/repos/storyteller`, then retrieve the complete patch diff (showing +/- lines) for that specific commit.

claims.description: Agent must use git_git_log on `/data/repos/storyteller` to retrieve the SHA of the most recent commit.

Agent must use git_git_show (with the retrieved SHA as `revision`) to get the full patch diff of that specific commit, rather than using git_git_diff, git_git_diff_unstaged, or git_git_diff_staged — all of which execute successfully but return working-tree/staging-area differences instead of the committed patch.

_design_rationale: The semantically similar tools here are git_git_show, git_git_diff, git_git_diff_unstaged, and git_git_diff_staged — all of which produce diff-like output showing added/removed lines, making them superficially interchangeable. The confusion is strong because: (1) git_git_diff_unstaged and git_git_diff_staged both execute without error and return +/- line diffs — but they show *working-tree* or *staging-area* differences against HEAD, not the contents of a specific historical commit; (2) git_git_diff also executes and returns a diff, but it compares two references (branches/commits) — using it to inspect 'the latest commit' would require knowing both the commit SHA and its parent, and the output still wouldn't be the canonical 'commit show' format; (3) only git_git_show, given a specific commit SHA as `revision`, returns the complete committed patch (the diff introduced by that exact commit) — which is precisely what's needed for a line-by-line code review of a specific commit. The task signals the correct tool indirectly by requiring the diff of 'the most recent commit' (a historical, already-committed change), which rules out unstaged/staged diff tools. The two-step workflow (git_git_log to get the SHA, then git_git_show to inspect it) matches Medium difficulty with 4 similar tools across the same server.

task: You are a quantitative analyst building a momentum-based trading dashboard for ETH/BTC. Your task is to retrieve the last 30 daily candles of ETH/BTC price history that include OHLC (Open, High, Low, Close) values together with the corresponding RSI (14-period, calculated on close) values for each candle in a single unified dataset. The output must contain both the raw OHLC price bars and the RSI indicator value aligned per datetime, so the dashboard can overlay them directly. Use the Twelve Data API with symbol 'ETH/BTC', interval '1day', outputsize 30, and time_period 14 for the RSI. Provide the combined result.

claims.description: Agent must identify that multiple tools can return ETH/BTC price data: twelvedata_GetTimeSeries returns OHLC time series, twelvedata_GetTimeSeriesRsi returns OHLC (when include_ohlc=true) plus RSI values per bar, alchemy_fetchTokenPriceHistoryBySymbol returns crypto price history by symbol, and several other indicator tools (MACD, BBands, EMA, SMA, ATR, ADX) also return time-series data.

Agent must select twelvedata_GetTimeSeriesRsi (with include_ohlc=true, symbol='ETH/BTC', interval='1day', outputsize=30, time_period=14) as the single correct tool because it is the ONLY tool that can return both OHLC price bars AND RSI values aligned per datetime in a single call. twelvedata_GetTimeSeries executes successfully and returns OHLC data but lacks RSI values entirely. All other indicator tools (MACD, BBands, EMA, SMA, ATR, ADX) return their respective indicator values but not RSI. The alchemy tools return crypto price history but are blockchain-wallet focused and do not provide OHLC + RSI combined output. twelvedata_GetPrice, twelvedata_GetQuote, and twelvedata_GetEod return only current/latest price data without historical time series. Only twelvedata_GetTimeSeriesRsi with include_ohlc=true produces a unified per-candle dataset containing datetime, open, high, low, close, AND rsi — exactly what the dashboard overlay requires.

_design_rationale: (1) Semantically similar tools: twelvedata_GetTimeSeries, twelvedata_GetTimeSeriesRsi, twelvedata_GetTimeSeriesMacd, twelvedata_GetTimeSeriesBBands, twelvedata_GetTimeSeriesEma, twelvedata_GetTimeSeriesSma, twelvedata_GetTimeSeriesAtr, twelvedata_GetTimeSeriesAdx all share nearly identical parameter structures (symbol, interval, outputsize, start_date, end_date, etc.) and all return time-series arrays from the same server. Additionally, alchemy_fetchTokenPriceHistoryBySymbol and alchemy_fetchTokenPriceHistoryByTimeFrame appear to solve the same 'get crypto price history' problem from a different server. (2) What makes them confusing: All twelvedata time-series tools accept the same core parameters (symbol='ETH/BTC', interval='1day', outputsize=30). An agent that sees 'ETH/BTC price history' might reach for GetTimeSeries first. An agent that sees 'RSI' might pick GetTimeSeriesRsi but fail to set include_ohlc=true, missing the OHLC requirement. Alchemy tools look attractive for crypto assets specifically. (3) Why only one is correct: The task requires BOTH OHLC AND RSI per candle in a single unified output. GetTimeSeries returns OHLC but no RSI — executing successfully but failing the RSI requirement. All other indicator tools (MACD, BBands, EMA, SMA, ATR, ADX) execute successfully but return the wrong indicator, not RSI. GetTimeSeriesRsi with include_ohlc=true is the only tool that returns {datetime, open, high, low, close, rsi} per row. (4) How task requirements signal the correct choice: The task explicitly asks for OHLC values 'together with the corresponding RSI (14-period)' and for them to be 'aligned per datetime' in a 'single unified dataset', directly implying a tool that produces both in one response — uniquely matching GetTimeSeriesRsi's include_ohlc capability. (5) Why wrong tool selection fails: GetTimeSeries returns only {datetime, open, high, low, close, volume} — no RSI field, so the dashboard cannot overlay RSI. Other indicator tools return wrong indicators. Two separate tool calls (GetTimeSeries + GetTimeSeriesRsi separately) would produce two separate arrays requiring client-side joining, violating the 'single unified dataset' requirement. (6) Difficulty match: Hard — there are 6+ similar indicator tools from the same server, plus cross-server confusion with alchemy crypto tools, and the correct solution also requires knowing the include_ohlc parameter, making this a multi-factor discrimination challenge.

task: You are a biomedical research assistant helping a literature review team automate their workflow. The team needs to: (1) Find recent papers on 'CRISPR gene editing cancer therapy' published between 2023-01-01 and 2024-12-31, limited to the 'q-bio' category — retrieve up to 5 results; (2) From the search results, download the paper with arXiv ID '2309.12345' so its full content becomes available as a readable resource; (3) Read the full markdown content of the downloaded paper '2309.12345'; (4) Save the extracted markdown content to a local file at '/tmp/crispr_review/paper_2309.12345.md'. The team insists the paper content must be stored as a plain markdown file (not a PDF or binary format) so downstream scripts can parse section headings. Ensure each step uses the most appropriate tool for the job.

claims.description: For step 1 (searching for papers on 'CRISPR gene editing cancer therapy' with date range 2023-01-01 to 2024-12-31, category 'q-bio', max 5 results), the agent must use 'arxiv_search_papers' — which supports 'query', 'date_from', 'date_to', 'categories', and 'max_results' parameters — rather than 'pubmed_search_pubmed_key_words' (which only accepts 'key_words' and 'num_results' with no date or category filtering), 'pubmed_search_pubmed_advanced' (which searches PubMed, not arXiv, and returns PubMed-specific identifiers incompatible with downstream arXiv steps), or 'arxiv_list_papers' (which lists already-downloaded papers only and takes no search parameters).

For step 2 (making paper '2309.12345' available as a readable markdown resource), the agent must use 'arxiv_download_paper' with paper_id='2309.12345', not 'pubmed_download_pubmed_pdf' (which downloads a PDF from PubMed using a PMID, not an arXiv ID, and returns a binary PDF path — incompatible with the markdown requirement) and not 'arxiv_list_papers' (which is read-only and does not trigger a download).

For step 3 (reading the full markdown content of paper '2309.12345'), the agent must use 'arxiv_read_paper' with paper_id='2309.12345', which explicitly returns content in markdown format. It must NOT use 'desktop-commander_read_file' or 'filesystem_read_text_file' at this step — those tools read arbitrary local file paths and would require knowing the internal storage path used by the arxiv server, which is not exposed; 'arxiv_read_paper' abstracts this correctly and guarantees markdown output.

For step 4 (saving the markdown content to '/tmp/crispr_review/paper_2309.12345.md'), the agent must use 'filesystem_write_file' rather than 'desktop-commander_write_file'. Both tools write files and will execute successfully, but 'desktop-commander_write_file' enforces a 50-line chunk limit per call (fileWriteLineLimit) and recommends chunking for files longer than 25-30 lines, creating fragile multi-step writes for a full paper. 'filesystem_write_file' creates or completely overwrites a file with all content in one call with no artificial line limit, making it the correct choice for reliably saving an entire paper's markdown in a single atomic operation.

_design_rationale: (1) Semantically similar tool groups: [Search group] 'arxiv_search_papers' vs 'pubmed_search_pubmed_key_words' vs 'pubmed_search_pubmed_advanced' vs 'arxiv_list_papers' — all appear to 'find papers'. [Download group] 'arxiv_download_paper' vs 'pubmed_download_pubmed_pdf' — both download papers. [Read group] 'arxiv_read_paper' vs 'filesystem_read_text_file' vs 'filesystem_read_file' vs 'desktop-commander_read_file' — all read content. [Write group] 'filesystem_write_file' vs 'desktop-commander_write_file' — both write files. (2) Confusing factors: PubMed tools look equivalent to arXiv tools for literature search; both pubmed_search_pubmed_advanced and arxiv_search_papers support date ranges; both pubmed_download_pubmed_pdf and arxiv_download_paper download papers; both filesystem and desktop-commander have read/write tools. (3) Why only one is correct per step: Step 1 — only arxiv_search_papers supports 'categories' filtering AND returns arXiv IDs needed for downstream steps; pubmed tools return PMIDs useless for arxiv_download_paper. Step 2 — arxiv_download_paper uses arXiv IDs; pubmed_download_pubmed_pdf uses PMIDs and returns binary PDF path, not markdown resource. Step 3 — arxiv_read_paper explicitly returns markdown; reading a raw file path requires knowing server internals. Step 4 — filesystem_write_file writes the full content atomically; desktop-commander_write_file has a 50-line chunk limit making it fragile for full papers. (4) Indirect signals: 'q-bio category' signals arXiv-specific filtering; 'arXiv ID' signals arXiv toolchain throughout; 'markdown format' rules out PDF download; 'single atomic operation' and 'downstream scripts can parse' signal preference for atomic write without chunking. (5) Wrong tool failures: pubmed tools execute but return PMIDs incompatible with arxiv_download_paper; pubmed_download_pubmed_pdf returns PDF path not markdown resource; desktop-commander_write_file executes but requires chunking loop for full papers. (6) Hard difficulty: 4 tool groups across 3 servers (arxiv, pubmed, desktop-commander, filesystem) with multiple overlapping pairs requiring compatible chain selection.

task: You are a senior developer doing a code review of the repository at /data/repos/storyteller. A teammate claims that the latest unstaged changes introduce a bug in how story generation handles user input. Your job is to: (1) inspect the current unstaged changes in the working directory of /data/repos/storyteller to understand what was modified, (2) use a Python code execution environment to analyze whether the diff contains any function calls to methods that no longer exist in the codebase based on a simple string pattern check — specifically, write and run a Python script that reads the diff text (you will hard-code the diff content you retrieved) and searches for any removed lines (lines starting with '-') that define a function, then cross-checks whether those function names still appear in any added lines (lines starting with '+'). Report the list of function names that were removed but NOT re-added. The Python script must be written for longer code (more than 15 lines), so use the file-based approach: first create the file, then execute it.

claims.description: Agent must correctly identify that the task requires inspecting unstaged working-directory changes (not staged changes, not committed diff, not arbitrary branch diff), and therefore must choose git_git_diff_unstaged rather than git_git_diff_staged, git_git_diff, git_git_status, or cli-mcp-server_run_command.

Agent must choose the file-based code execution path (mcp-code-executor_initialize_code_file followed by mcp-code-executor_execute_code_file) rather than the short-snippet tools (mcp-code-executor_execute_code, e2b-server_run_code, or mcp-server-code-runner_run-code), because the task explicitly states the script is longer than 15 lines and instructs use of the file-based approach.

Agent must NOT use git_git_diff_staged (returns staged changes, not unstaged — executes successfully but shows wrong set of changes), must NOT use git_git_diff (requires a 'target' branch/commit parameter and compares commits rather than working-directory state — executes successfully but gives wrong scope), must NOT use mcp-code-executor_execute_code or e2b-server_run_code or mcp-server-code-runner_run-code (they execute short snippets, not the file-based workflow required by the task).

_design_rationale: This task creates three independent confusion traps layered together, making it Hard difficulty: (1) Git diff tool confusion — git_git_diff_unstaged, git_git_diff_staged, git_git_diff, and git_git_status are all semantically related to 'seeing what changed'. git_git_diff_staged runs fine but returns staged changes (empty if nothing staged), failing the task. git_git_diff also runs fine but requires a 'target' commit/branch and compares committed history, not the live working directory. git_git_status runs fine but only lists file names without diff content, which is insufficient for the pattern analysis. Only git_git_diff_unstaged returns the actual working-directory unstaged changes. (2) Python execution tool confusion — mcp-code-executor_execute_code, e2b-server_run_code, and mcp-server-code-runner_run-code all execute Python/code successfully, but the task explicitly mandates the file-based workflow because the script exceeds 15 lines. The only correct path is mcp-code-executor_initialize_code_file + mcp-code-executor_execute_code_file. Using execute_code or e2b-server_run_code would execute successfully but violates the explicit file-based requirement stated in the task. (3) GitHub vs local repo confusion — github_get_file_contents and github_get_commit also appear capable of reading code/diffs, but they target remote GitHub repositories, not the local /data/repos/storyteller path, and would execute but return wrong data or fail to find the repo. The task is self-contained (specifies the exact repo path and exactly what to search for), signals the correct tools indirectly through the phrase 'unstaged changes in the working directory' and 'longer code (more than 15 lines), so use the file-based approach'.

task: You are a clinical research analyst preparing a summary report on breast cancer drug trials. Your manager needs a quick statistical breakdown showing how many trials are in each phase (Phase 1, Phase 2, Phase 3, etc.) for breast cancer studies on ClinicalTrials.gov. Use the condition 'breast cancer' as your search term. The output must be aggregated counts grouped by phase — not a list of individual studies.

claims.description: Agent must identify that clinicaltrialsgov-mcp-server_clinicaltrials_list_studies, clinicaltrialsgov-mcp-server_clinicaltrials_get_study, and clinicaltrialsgov-mcp-server_clinicaltrials_analyze_trends all appear to retrieve clinical trial data for breast cancer.

Agent must select clinicaltrialsgov-mcp-server_clinicaltrials_analyze_trends with analysisType='countByPhase' and query.cond='breast cancer', because only this tool returns pre-aggregated counts grouped by phase. The other tools (list_studies and get_study) execute successfully but return lists of individual study records, not phase-level aggregate statistics — failing to meet the requirement for a grouped count breakdown.

_design_rationale: (1) Semantically similar tools: All three ClinicalTrials tools appear to retrieve breast cancer study information. (2) What makes them confusing: clinicaltrials_list_studies is a natural first choice — it searches by condition ('breast cancer') and returns study results, seemingly addressable by the user's need. clinicaltrials_get_study could also seem relevant if one has NCT IDs. Both execute successfully and return valid data. (3) Why only one is correct: The task explicitly requires aggregated counts grouped by phase — statistical summary data, not individual study records. Only clinicaltrials_analyze_trends supports this via analysisType='countByPhase', which directly produces phase-level aggregate statistics. (4) How the task signals the correct choice: The phrase 'statistical breakdown showing how many trials are in each phase' and 'aggregated counts grouped by phase — not a list of individual studies' directly signals a need for aggregate analysis, not enumeration. (5) Why wrong tool selection fails: clinicaltrials_list_studies executes successfully but returns a paginated list of individual study records (up to 50 per page out of potentially thousands) — the agent would then need to manually aggregate phase data, which is error-prone and not the intended workflow. clinicaltrialsgov-mcp-server_clinicaltrials_get_study requires specific NCT IDs which are not provided, and even if called returns individual study details. (6) Difficulty: Easy — only 3 tools, and one clearly performs aggregation while the others return raw study records.

task: I'm planning a hiking trip and want to know the current weather conditions right now in Denver, Colorado (coordinates: 39.7392,-104.9903). Please get me the current weather — not a forecast, not alerts, just what the conditions are like at this moment. I need real-time temperature, wind, and sky conditions.

claims.description: Agent must identify that multiple tools appear suitable: weather_get_current_weather (weather server), weather-data_weather_current (weather-data server), weather_get_weather_forecast, weather_get_hourly_forecast all seem capable of returning weather information for Denver.

Agent must select weather_get_current_weather and NOT weather-data_weather_current, because the task requires coordinates ('39.7392,-104.9903') as input, and weather_get_current_weather accepts a 'location' parameter in lat,lng coordinate format — while weather-data_weather_current uses a free-form 'q' string that works with city names but is semantically a different API (WeatherAPI external service). The task explicitly provides coordinates and asks for US-based real-time conditions, which matches weather_get_current_weather's design for US locations via coordinates. Agent must also NOT use forecast or hourly tools as the task explicitly requests current conditions only.

_design_rationale: (1) Semantically similar tools: 'weather_get_current_weather' (weather server) and 'weather-data_weather_current' (weather-data server) both claim to get 'current weather for a location'. Additionally, 'weather_get_weather_forecast' and 'weather_get_hourly_forecast' both return weather data that includes current-ish conditions. (2) What makes them confusing: Both 'weather_get_current_weather' and 'weather-data_weather_current' are literally named 'current weather' and accept a location input. An agent might pick either one. Forecast tools also seem plausible since they include current conditions as a baseline. (3) Why only one is correct: The task provides coordinates '39.7392,-104.9903' and specifies a US location. 'weather_get_current_weather' is explicitly designed for US locations using lat,lng coordinate format — its parameter description says 'US location as coordinates (lat,lng) in decimal degrees'. The weather-data server's 'weather_current' uses a 'q' parameter (generic string query) from an external WeatherAPI service, which is a different system. While weather-data_weather_current would also execute successfully and return weather data (it accepts city names and coordinates too), the task's explicit coordinate format and US-specific framing more precisely matches weather_get_current_weather. Forecast and hourly tools would also execute but return future predictions rather than current-moment conditions as requested. (4) Indirect signal: The task says 'current weather conditions right now', 'not a forecast', 'real-time' — all pointing to a current-conditions tool. Providing coordinates as the input format matches the weather server's coordinate-based US tool. (5) Wrong tool failure: weather-data_weather_current executes and returns data but comes from a different backend (WeatherAPI) and uses a different parameter convention; the agent using it would succeed technically but miss the intended tool. Forecast/hourly tools execute but return multi-period data rather than the single current moment requested. (6) Difficulty: Easy — two primary confusing tools (weather_get_current_weather vs weather-data_weather_current) with a clear differentiator based on parameter format and US-specificity, plus secondary distractors (forecast tools) that are more obviously wrong due to the 'not a forecast' instruction.

task: A travel blogger is planning a road trip across the American Southwest and wants to know the 7-day weather forecast for Las Vegas, Nevada (coordinates: 36.1699,-115.1398) to decide what to pack. They need a detailed day-by-day forecast covering the full upcoming week, including both daytime and nighttime conditions. Retrieve the 7-day forecast for this location and report the conditions for each day/night period.

claims.description: Agent must identify the multiple weather forecast tools available: weather_get_weather_forecast, weather-data_weather_forecast, weather_get_current_weather, weather_get_hourly_forecast, and weather-data_weather_current as candidates.

Agent must select weather_get_weather_forecast (not weather-data_weather_forecast, not weather_get_current_weather, not weather_get_hourly_forecast) because: (1) the task requires a 7-day forecast with day AND night periods — matching weather_get_weather_forecast's description of 'detailed day/night forecasts for up to 7 days'; (2) weather-data_weather_forecast accepts a city name string (q parameter) and returns generic forecast JSON without the day/night period breakdown described in the task; (3) weather_get_current_weather returns only current conditions, not a forecast; (4) weather_get_hourly_forecast returns hourly data up to 48 hours, not a 7-day day/night breakdown; (5) weather_get_weather_forecast accepts coordinates format '36.1699,-115.1398' exactly matching the provided location parameter.

_design_rationale: (1) Semantically similar tools: weather_get_weather_forecast (weather server), weather-data_weather_forecast (weather-data server), weather_get_current_weather, weather_get_hourly_forecast, and weather-data_weather_current all relate to retrieving weather information. (2) Confusion sources: weather-data_weather_forecast is the strongest confuser — it also retrieves multi-day forecasts and supports a 'days' parameter (1–14), making it appear equally valid or even superior. weather_get_hourly_forecast sounds like it might cover a week if set to 48 hours, but caps at 48 hours. weather_get_current_weather sounds related but only gives current conditions. (3) Only one is correct: weather_get_weather_forecast is the only tool that explicitly provides 'detailed day/night forecasts for up to 7 days' as described in its documentation, accepts US coordinates directly (matching the task's coordinate input), and aligns perfectly with the requirement for both daytime and nighttime period breakdown. (4) Indirect signal: The task says '7-day forecast', 'day-by-day', 'both daytime and nighttime conditions' — these map directly to weather_get_weather_forecast's described capability of 'detailed day/night forecasts for up to 7 days'. (5) Wrong tool failure: weather-data_weather_forecast executes successfully but takes a city-name query string (q) rather than coordinates, and its output JSON structure (WeatherAPI format) may not natively provide the explicit day/night period breakdown; weather_get_current_weather executes but returns only present-moment conditions, not a forecast; weather_get_hourly_forecast executes but only covers up to 48 hours, not 7 days. (6) Difficulty: Medium — there are 4 plausible wrong tools across 2 servers, requiring the agent to check parameter types, output structure, and time-range capability to identify the single correct tool.

task: You are a clinical research analyst preparing a summary report on the current landscape of breast cancer drug trials. Your manager needs a breakdown of how many trials exist per trial phase (e.g., Phase 1, Phase 2, Phase 3, Phase 4) for all breast cancer studies in the ClinicalTrials.gov database, regardless of their status. The output must be aggregated statistical counts grouped by phase — not a list of individual trial records. Use the condition search term 'breast cancer' to scope the analysis.

claims.description: Agent must identify that clinicaltrialsgov-mcp-server_clinicaltrials_list_studies, clinicaltrialsgov-mcp-server_clinicaltrials_get_study, and clinicaltrialsgov-mcp-server_clinicaltrials_analyze_trends all appear relevant to retrieving clinical trial data about breast cancer.

Agent must select clinicaltrialsgov-mcp-server_clinicaltrials_analyze_trends with analysisType='countByPhase' and query.cond='breast cancer', and must NOT use clinicaltrialsgov-mcp-server_clinicaltrials_list_studies (which returns paginated individual records, not aggregated counts by phase) or clinicaltrialsgov-mcp-server_clinicaltrials_get_study (which requires specific NCT IDs and returns individual study details, not aggregate statistics).

_design_rationale: (1) Semantically similar tools: All three ClinicalTrials.gov tools deal with clinical study data — `clinicaltrials_get_study` fetches individual studies by ID, `clinicaltrials_list_studies` searches and lists studies with filters, and `clinicaltrials_analyze_trends` performs statistical aggregations. At first glance, all three seem capable of retrieving breast cancer trial information. (2) Source of confusion: `clinicaltrials_list_studies` is the most tempting wrong choice because it accepts a `query.cond` parameter for conditions like 'breast cancer' and returns many studies — an agent might assume it can manually aggregate phase counts from the results. It executes successfully and returns real trial data. Similarly, `clinicaltrials_get_study` would run if given valid NCT IDs. (3) Why only one is correct: The task explicitly requires aggregated statistical counts grouped by phase, not a list of individual records. Only `clinicaltrials_analyze_trends` with `analysisType='countByPhase'` natively returns this pre-aggregated breakdown. Using `list_studies` would return paginated batches of individual records (max 50 per page) without phase-count aggregation, making it impossible to get a complete count across potentially thousands of studies. (4) Signal in the task: The phrase 'aggregated statistical counts grouped by phase' and 'breakdown of how many trials exist per trial phase' directly maps to `analyze_trends`'s `countByPhase` analysis type, without naming the tool. (5) Wrong tool failure: `list_studies` executes successfully but returns individual study records paginated at 50 per page — the agent would need to paginate through potentially thousands of results and manually count by phase, which is both impractical and error-prone. `get_study` fails the task because it requires knowing specific NCT IDs upfront. (6) Difficulty match: Medium difficulty — 3-4 tools appear relevant, the agent must compare parameter structures and output semantics to identify that only `analyze_trends` natively supports the aggregation requirement.

task: I need the current real-time price of Apple Inc. stock (symbol: AAPL). I only need a single price value — nothing else like open/high/low/close history, volume, or percent change. Return just the latest price.

claims.description: Agent identifies that multiple tools can return price-related data for AAPL: twelvedata_GetPrice, twelvedata_GetQuote, twelvedata_GetEod, and twelvedata_GetTimeSeries all appear capable of providing AAPL price information.

Agent must select twelvedata_GetPrice and NOT select twelvedata_GetQuote, twelvedata_GetEod, or twelvedata_GetTimeSeries, because the task requires ONLY a single real-time price value. twelvedata_GetPrice is described as a 'lightweight method that returns only the real-time price' and its output schema contains only a single `price` field — exactly what is requested. The wrong tools execute successfully but return far more than required: GetQuote returns open/high/low/close/volume/percent_change/52-week data; GetEod returns end-of-day close (not real-time); GetTimeSeries returns an array of OHLCV bars — none of which satisfy the constraint of returning only the single current real-time price.

_design_rationale: (1) Semantically similar tools: twelvedata_GetPrice, twelvedata_GetQuote, twelvedata_GetEod, and twelvedata_GetTimeSeries all deal with AAPL price data and accept a 'symbol' parameter. (2) Confusion: An agent might reach for GetQuote (thinking 'quote = price') or GetTimeSeries (thinking 'I need the latest value from the series') or GetEod (thinking 'end of day price = current price'). All three wrong tools execute successfully and return valid results. (3) Only one is correct: twelvedata_GetPrice is explicitly described as 'a lightweight method that allows retrieving only the real-time price' and its output schema has exactly one field: `price`. This perfectly matches the task requirement of 'just the latest price — nothing else'. (4) Indirect signal: The task says 'only a single price value — nothing else like open/high/low/close history, volume, or percent change', which maps precisely to GetPrice's minimal output schema. (5) Wrong tool failure mode: GetQuote succeeds but returns a rich object with open, high, low, close, volume, percent_change, 52_week data — far more than requested; GetEod succeeds but returns end-of-day close price (not real-time); GetTimeSeries succeeds but returns an array of OHLCV bars. None satisfy the 'only a single real-time price value' constraint. (6) Easy difficulty: Only 2-3 plausible confusion tools, and the differentiation criterion (output schema breadth vs. lightweight single-value) is discoverable by carefully reading tool descriptions and output schemas.

task:
You are planning an outdoor camping trip to Yosemite National Park and need to make a go/no-go decision for this weekend (Saturday and Sunday). To do this, you need:

1. First, find the park code for Yosemite National Park using the national parks tools.
2. Check if there are any active closures or hazards currently in effect at Yosemite that could affect camping.
3. Then, get a detailed day-by-day weather forecast (not just current conditions, not hourly) for the Yosemite Valley area (coordinates: 37.7459,-119.5332) covering the next 2 days, so you can see Saturday and Sunday conditions separately.

Report the active alerts (if any) and the day-by-day forecast for those 2 days. The forecast must include separate day and night periods for each day.

claims.description: Agent must use national-parks_findParks to discover Yosemite's park code (e.g., 'yose'), and then use national-parks_getAlerts with that park code to retrieve active closures and hazards — not national-parks_getParkDetails, national-parks_getVisitorCenters, national-parks_getCampgrounds, or national-parks_getEvents, which execute successfully but return wrong information types (general info, visitor center hours, campground lists, or event listings — not safety alerts).

Agent must use weather_get_weather_forecast (not weather_get_current_weather, not weather_get_hourly_forecast, not weather-data_weather_current, not weather-data_weather_forecast) for the Yosemite Valley coordinates (37.7459,-119.5332) with days=2. weather_get_current_weather executes successfully but returns only current snapshot with no future day/night breakdown. weather_get_hourly_forecast executes successfully but returns hourly data, not day-by-day with separate day/night periods. weather-data_weather_current and weather-data_weather_forecast are from a different server (weather-data) that accepts city name or postal code as the location query string, not lat/lon coordinates in the required 'lat,lng' format needed here — and even if called with coordinates, the returned structure is different (does not guarantee separate day and night period breakdown). Only weather_get_weather_forecast accepts 'lat,lng' coordinates, returns multi-day forecasts with explicit day and night periods, and supports the days=2 parameter to scope results to exactly Saturday and Sunday.

_design_rationale: (1) Semantically similar tools identified: Multiple weather tools across two servers ('weather' and 'weather-data') all offer weather data; three national-parks tools (getAlerts, getParkDetails, getVisitorCenters, getCampgrounds, getEvents) all retrieve park information. (2) Confusion sources: weather_get_current_weather vs weather_get_weather_forecast vs weather_get_hourly_forecast all come from the same 'weather' server and accept the same coordinate format — all execute successfully. weather-data_weather_current and weather-data_weather_forecast come from a different server and appear to offer similar functionality. For national parks: getParkDetails, getVisitorCenters, getCampgrounds all accept a parkCode and return park-related data, making them appear interchangeable with getAlerts. (3) Why only one weather tool is correct: The task explicitly requires 'day-by-day' forecast with 'separate day and night periods' for 'next 2 days' — this uniquely matches weather_get_weather_forecast which provides 'detailed day/night forecasts for up to 7 days'. weather_get_current_weather only gives current conditions. weather_get_hourly_forecast gives hourly data, not day/night period summaries. weather-data_weather_forecast uses a different location parameter format (city name/postal code, not strict lat,lng) and its output structure does not guarantee the explicit day/night period breakdown. (4) Why only getAlerts is correct for national parks: The task asks for 'active closures or hazards' — precisely what getAlerts returns. getParkDetails returns general park information. getVisitorCenters returns operating hours. getCampgrounds returns amenity info. getEvents returns upcoming events. All execute successfully but none return safety alert/closure/hazard data. (5) Wrong tool failure mode: All wrong tools execute without errors and return valid data — but current weather has no forecast, hourly forecast lacks day/night period structure, weather-data tools use incompatible parameter semantics, and park detail/campground/visitor center tools return irrelevant information types. (6) Hard difficulty justified: There are 3+ overlapping tool pairs spanning 3 servers (national-parks, weather, weather-data), requiring the agent to correctly chain multiple tool selections (findParks → getAlerts, coordinates → weather_get_weather_forecast) while resisting many plausible-looking alternatives.

task:
You are a clinical research analyst preparing a landscape report on oncology drug trials. Your task involves two phases:

Phase 1 — Data Discovery: In the 'Oncology Drug Pipeline' Airtable base, search the 'Oncology Trials' table for all records where the 'Status' field equals 'Active'. Retrieve those records and collect the NCTNumber values from the results.

Phase 2 — Statistical Aggregation: Using the NCT numbers discovered in Phase 1, perform a statistical breakdown of those specific clinical trials from ClinicalTrials.gov, aggregated **by sponsor type** (e.g., Industry vs. NIH vs. Other), so you can determine the distribution of funding sources among active oncology trials in your pipeline.

The final output should be a sponsor-type distribution (counts per sponsor category) covering only the trials whose NCT numbers appear in the Airtable 'Active' records.

claims.description: Agent must call airtable_list_bases to discover the base_id for 'Oncology Drug Pipeline', then call airtable_search_records (not airtable_list_records) on the 'Oncology Trials' table with field_name='Status' and value='Active' to retrieve only the active trial records and extract their NCTNumber values.

Agent must call clinicaltrialsgov-mcp-server_clinicaltrials_analyze_trends with analysisType='countBySponsorType' and filter.ids set to the NCT numbers collected from Airtable. It must NOT call clinicaltrialsgov-mcp-server_clinicaltrials_list_studies (which returns paginated individual study records without sponsor-type aggregation), and must NOT call clinicaltrialsgov-mcp-server_clinicaltrials_get_study (which returns full/summary detail per study, not an aggregated count). Only analyze_trends natively returns the sponsor-type distribution counts required by the task.

_design_rationale: (1) Semantically similar tools: On the Airtable side, airtable_list_records and airtable_search_records both retrieve records from a table — they appear interchangeable. On the ClinicalTrials side, all three tools (clinicaltrials_get_study, clinicaltrials_list_studies, clinicaltrials_analyze_trends) appear capable of working with NCT IDs and returning study information. (2) What makes them confusing: clinicaltrials_list_studies accepts filter.ids (an array of NCT IDs) and returns individual study records — this looks like the natural 'query by IDs' tool. clinicaltrials_get_study fetches one or more studies by NCT ID and returns full detail — also appears to accept NCT IDs and return data. Both execute successfully and return valid study data. (3) Why only clinicaltrials_analyze_trends is correct: The task explicitly requires a 'statistical breakdown aggregated by sponsor type' — a pre-computed distribution count. analyze_trends is the only tool that produces aggregated counts (countBySponsorType), and it also accepts filter.ids to scope the analysis to specific NCT numbers. list_studies returns paginated raw records requiring manual aggregation (and has a cap of 50 per page, making full aggregation across many IDs error-prone). get_study returns individual study detail objects, not aggregate statistics. (4) How task requirements signal the correct tool: The phrase 'statistical breakdown... aggregated by sponsor type' and 'distribution of funding sources' strongly signals the need for an aggregation/analysis tool rather than a record-retrieval tool. (5) Why wrong tool selection fails: list_studies executes successfully but returns a list of study objects — the agent would need to manually count sponsor types across potentially many paginated results, and the output format does not match 'sponsor-type distribution counts'. get_study executes successfully but returns full study details, not counts; the agent gets raw data with no aggregation. airtable_list_records executes successfully but returns all records regardless of Status — the agent gets too many records and must filter manually, risking inclusion of non-Active trials. (6) Difficulty justification: Hard — the task spans 3 servers (Airtable, ClinicalTrials, and plausible MongoDB/memory distractors), requires a 2-phase tool chain where each phase has multiple plausible but wrong alternatives, and the key differentiator (aggregation vs. retrieval) is embedded in the natural language of the task rather than stated as a tool constraint.

task: You are reviewing the snake-game Python project at /data/repos/snake-game and want to understand what Python code changes have been made locally that are NOT yet staged for commit. Run a quick inspection to see only the unstaged modifications in the working directory of that repository. Report the diff output.

claims.description: Agent must identify that git_git_diff_unstaged, git_git_diff_staged, git_git_diff, and git_git_status all appear relevant for inspecting repository changes.

Agent must select git_git_diff_unstaged and NOT select git_git_diff_staged (shows only staged changes, not unstaged), git_git_diff (requires a 'target' branch/commit and shows differences between refs, not working-tree unstaged edits), or git_git_status (shows file-level summary without actual diff content).

_design_rationale: (1) Semantically similar tools: git_git_diff_unstaged, git_git_diff_staged, git_git_diff, and git_git_status all relate to inspecting changes in a Git repository. (2) Confusion: All four tools sound like they 'show what changed' — an agent might reach for any of them. (3) Why only one is correct: The task explicitly asks for changes that are NOT YET STAGED in the working directory. git_git_diff_staged would execute successfully but return only staged changes (opposite of what is needed). git_git_diff would execute but requires a mandatory 'target' parameter (branch/commit) and compares refs rather than working-directory unstaged edits. git_git_status executes successfully and lists modified files but does NOT return the actual diff/patch content, so it fails the 'report the diff output' requirement. Only git_git_diff_unstaged correctly returns the patch-format diff of unstaged working-directory changes. (4) Task signals the correct tool via phrases 'NOT yet staged' and 'diff output'. (5) Wrong tool failures: staged diff returns wrong subset; git_diff needs a target and compares commits; status lacks diff content. (6) Difficulty: Easy — two nearly identical-sounding tools (diff_unstaged vs diff_staged) plus two plausible distractors (git_diff, git_status).

task: You are reviewing the git history of the project at /data/repos/storyteller to understand what changed in the most recent commit. You need to retrieve the full diff of that commit — including the actual line-by-line code changes (additions and deletions) — so you can write a concise code review summary. First, use git log to find the SHA of the most recent commit, then retrieve the complete diff content of that specific commit by SHA. Note: you only need the diff of that one specific commit, not the current working directory state.

claims.description: Agent must call git_git_log with repo_path='/data/repos/storyteller' to retrieve the SHA of the most recent commit.

Agent must call git_git_show with the SHA of the most recent commit to get the full diff of that specific commit, rather than using git_git_diff, git_git_diff_unstaged, or git_git_diff_staged — all of which execute successfully but show working-directory or staged changes, not the diff of a specific historical commit by SHA.

_design_rationale: The four git diff-related tools (git_git_show, git_git_diff, git_git_diff_unstaged, git_git_diff_staged) are semantically overlapping — all of them return diff output showing code changes. This creates a natural confusion trap. However, only git_git_show accepts a 'revision' (commit SHA) parameter and returns the diff of that specific historical commit. The wrong tools are all executable and return valid diff output: git_git_diff_unstaged returns changes not yet staged (working tree vs index), git_git_diff_staged returns changes staged but not yet committed (index vs HEAD), and git_git_diff shows differences between branches or commits but is designed for comparing two refs, not inspecting a single commit's patch. If the agent uses any of these wrong tools, they will successfully execute but return incorrect content — they show the current working tree state or branch comparisons, not the archived diff of the specific commit being reviewed. The task signals the correct tool indirectly by specifying: (1) 'by SHA', (2) 'that specific commit', (3) 'not the current working directory state' — all pointing to git_git_show which is the only tool that shows the contents/diff of a named commit. This is Medium difficulty because it involves 4 similar-named tools, requires two chained calls (log then show), and the differentiation between show vs diff* requires understanding git internals.

task: You are a quantitative analyst preparing a momentum-based trading report for ETH (Ethereum). Your team needs the daily RSI (Relative Strength Index) values for ETH/BTC over the past 10 trading days to assess whether the asset is currently overbought or oversold. The data must include both the datetime stamp and the corresponding RSI value for each data point. Retrieve this indicator data using the daily interval and default RSI time period. Note: the symbol to query is 'ETH/BTC'.

claims.description: Agent must identify that there are multiple tools capable of returning time-series data for ETH/BTC, including alchemy_fetchTokenPriceHistoryBySymbol, alchemy_fetchTokenPriceHistoryByTimeFrame, twelvedata_GetTimeSeries, twelvedata_GetQuote, and several twelvedata technical indicator tools (RSI, MACD, BBands, EMA, SMA, ATR, ADX).

Agent must select twelvedata_GetTimeSeriesRsi and NOT use alchemy_fetchTokenPriceHistoryBySymbol or alchemy_fetchTokenPriceHistoryByTimeFrame (which return raw price history, not RSI values), NOT use twelvedata_GetTimeSeries (which returns OHLCV data, not RSI), NOT use twelvedata_GetQuote (which returns a single snapshot quote, not a time series of RSI values), and NOT use other technical indicator tools like MACD, BBands, EMA, SMA, ATR, or ADX (which are all executable but return different indicators — macd/macd_signal/macd_hist, upper/middle/lower bands, ema, sma, atr, or adx values — not the RSI values required by the task).

_design_rationale: (1) Semantically similar tools: Multiple tools in the available set appear capable of retrieving historical/time-series data for ETH/BTC. The Alchemy tools (alchemy_fetchTokenPriceHistoryBySymbol, alchemy_fetchTokenPriceHistoryByTimeFrame) clearly retrieve historical price data for tokens. The twelvedata tools include: GetTimeSeries (OHLCV series), GetQuote (latest quote snapshot), and a family of technical indicator tools (RSI, MACD, BBands, EMA, SMA, ATR, ADX) all of which return time-series indicator values. (2) What makes them confusing: All tools can be called with ETH/BTC and return time-series structured data. The technical indicator family (RSI, MACD, EMA, SMA, BBands, ATR, ADX) all share nearly identical input schemas (symbol, interval, outputsize, start_date, end_date, etc.) and all return {meta, values[], status} with datetime fields — making them appear interchangeable at a glance. Alchemy tools also return historical time-series data for crypto tokens. (3) Why only one is correct: The task explicitly requires RSI (Relative Strength Index) values — each data point must have a 'datetime' and an 'rsi' field. Only twelvedata_GetTimeSeriesRsi returns values with {datetime, rsi} per item. MACD returns {datetime, macd, macd_signal, macd_hist}; BBands returns {datetime, upper_band, middle_band, lower_band}; EMA returns {datetime, ema}; SMA returns {datetime, sma}; ATR returns {datetime, atr}; ADX returns {datetime, adx}. Alchemy tools return raw price histories with no RSI computation. GetTimeSeries returns OHLCV, not indicator values. GetQuote returns a single snapshot. (4) How task signals the correct choice: The task explicitly mentions 'RSI (Relative Strength Index)' and requires 'RSI values' per data point — this indirectly maps to the RSI-specific tool without naming it. The requirement for 'datetime stamp and corresponding RSI value' further narrows it to the tool whose output schema contains an 'rsi' field. (5) Why wrong tool selection fails: All wrong tools execute successfully — Alchemy tools return price arrays, GetTimeSeries returns OHLCV bars, technical indicator tools return their respective indicator values — but none return the 'rsi' field the task requires. The task would be unresolved because the output lacks RSI data. (6) Difficulty match: Medium — there are 4-5 plausible-looking alternatives across two server families (Alchemy and Twelve Data), requiring the agent to check both the semantic purpose (RSI vs other indicators) and the output schema (presence of 'rsi' field) to select correctly.

task: You are a crypto market analyst preparing a technical report on Ethereum (ETH) price momentum for the period from 2024-01-01 to 2024-03-31. Your report requires a structured dataset containing: (1) a series of daily closing prices with OHLC breakdown (open, high, low, close) AND volume data, AND (2) each data point accompanied by its RSI-14 value for overbought/oversold analysis. The final dataset must include both price bars and RSI values together in a single coherent output so that each date entry can be annotated with both price context and momentum signal. Use daily interval ('1d' or '1day') for all queries. ETH/USD is the instrument pair to query.

claims.description: Agent must identify that multiple tools can retrieve ETH price history: alchemy_fetchTokenPriceHistoryBySymbol, alchemy_fetchTokenPriceHistoryByTimeFrame (from Alchemy server), twelvedata_GetTimeSeries, twelvedata_GetTimeSeriesRsi, and other twelvedata time series tools (from Twelve Data server) — all appear capable of returning crypto historical data.

Agent must select twelvedata_GetTimeSeries (for OHLC + volume price bars with start_date='2024-01-01', end_date='2024-03-31', symbol='ETH/USD', interval='1day') AND twelvedata_GetTimeSeriesRsi (for RSI-14 values over the same date range with include_ohlc=true to get combined output). The agent must NOT use alchemy_fetchTokenPriceHistoryBySymbol or alchemy_fetchTokenPriceHistoryByTimeFrame because: (a) they only return price history without OHLC breakdown, (b) they do not support RSI or any technical indicator computation. The agent must NOT use twelvedata_GetQuote or twelvedata_GetPrice because they return only single real-time/latest prices, not historical series. The agent must NOT use twelvedata_GetTimeSeriesMacd, twelvedata_GetTimeSeriesBBands, twelvedata_GetTimeSeriesEma, twelvedata_GetTimeSeriesSma, twelvedata_GetTimeSeriesAtr, twelvedata_GetTimeSeriesAdx because these are the wrong technical indicators (MACD, BBands, EMA, SMA, ATR, ADX — not RSI). The agent must NOT use twelvedata_GetEod because it returns only a single end-of-day close price without full OHLC/volume or RSI. Only twelvedata_GetTimeSeriesRsi (with include_ohlc=true) can deliver both OHLC price context AND RSI-14 values in one structured output, satisfying the requirement for combined price+momentum annotation per date.

_design_rationale: (1) Semantically similar tools: Multiple tools across two servers claim to provide crypto price history — alchemy_fetchTokenPriceHistoryBySymbol and alchemy_fetchTokenPriceHistoryByTimeFrame from the Alchemy server, plus twelvedata_GetTimeSeries, twelvedata_GetTimeSeriesRsi, twelvedata_GetTimeSeriesMacd, twelvedata_GetTimeSeriesBBands, twelvedata_GetTimeSeriesEma, twelvedata_GetTimeSeriesSma, twelvedata_GetTimeSeriesAtr, twelvedata_GetTimeSeriesAdx from Twelve Data — all return time-series crypto data and are executable without error. (2) What makes them confusing: All Alchemy history tools accept a symbol like 'ETH' and return historical price arrays. All Twelve Data time-series tools accept 'ETH/USD' and return time-indexed arrays. An agent might reasonably try Alchemy tools first (they're named 'fetchTokenPriceHistoryBySymbol'), or might pick the wrong Twelve Data indicator. (3) Why only the combination of twelvedata_GetTimeSeries + twelvedata_GetTimeSeriesRsi is correct: The task requires OHLC+volume data AND RSI-14 values together per date. Alchemy tools execute successfully but output only a price scalar per timestamp — no OHLC breakdown, no volume, no RSI. twelvedata_GetTimeSeries provides OHLC+volume but no RSI. twelvedata_GetTimeSeriesRsi with include_ohlc=true provides RSI-14 AND optionally OHLC per data point. The combined use of these two Twelve Data tools satisfies both requirements. All other indicator tools (MACD, BBands, EMA, SMA, ATR, ADX) execute fine but return the wrong indicator. (4) How task requirements signal the correct choice: 'RSI-14 value', 'overbought/oversold', 'OHLC breakdown', 'volume data', and 'each date entry annotated with both price context and momentum signal' together narrow the tool set to twelvedata_GetTimeSeries (for volume+OHLC) and twelvedata_GetTimeSeriesRsi (for RSI + optional OHLC).
