(2) Confusion source: All four tools appear to 'find papers' at first glance. pubmed_search_pubmed_advanced is especially deceptive because it has date range parameters (start_date, end_date) similar to arxiv_search_papers's date_from/date_to, making it look like a valid alternative. pubmed_search_pubmed_key_words also accepts a keyword query. arxiv_list_papers sounds like it could list/search arXiv papers. (3) Only one is correct: The task explicitly requires (a) arXiv as the source (q-bio category is arXiv-specific — PubMed has no such category), (b) category filtering support, and (c) date range filtering — all in one call. Only arxiv_search_papers has all three: query, date_from, date_to, and categories parameters targeting arXiv. (4) Indirect signal: The task mentions 'q-bio category on arXiv' and requires a single call with date + category filters — this uniquely matches arxiv_search_papers without naming it. (5) Wrong tool failure analysis: pubmed_search_pubmed_key_words executes successfully but searches PubMed (wrong database), has no category or date filter support; pubmed_search_pubmed_advanced executes successfully with date filters but searches PubMed not arXiv, and has no 'categories' parameter for arXiv-specific categories; arxiv_list_papers executes successfully but only lists already-downloaded local papers with no query/filter parameters at all — it cannot search the arXiv API. (6) Medium difficulty: 4 tools across 2 servers (arXiv + PubMed) with overlapping semantics, requiring the agent to check parameter structure (categories field), database scope (arXiv vs PubMed), and capability (search vs list) to identify the unique correct tool.

task: A quantitative analyst at a hedge fund is building a momentum trading dashboard for Ethereum (ETH). She needs to assess whether ETH is currently overbought or oversold by retrieving the last 30 daily RSI values for 'ETH/BTC' with a 14-period lookback. The output must include both the RSI time series values (with datetime and rsi fields per data point) AND the indicator metadata (name, series_type, time_period) in the same response object, so the dashboard can auto-label charts. Retrieve this data now.

claims.description: Agent must recognize that 'RSI' (Relative Strength Index) is a specific momentum oscillator, distinguishing it from other technical indicators like MACD (momentum but different output), BBands (volatility), EMA/SMA (trend smoothing), ATR (volatility range), and ADX (trend strength) — all of which return time series but with completely different indicator fields (macd/macd_signal/macd_hist, upper_band/middle_band/lower_band, ema, sma, atr, adx) instead of the required 'rsi' field.

Agent must select twelvedata_GetTimeSeriesRsi and NOT select twelvedata_GetTimeSeries (returns OHLCV raw price data with no RSI field at all), twelvedata_GetTimeSeriesMacd (executes successfully returning macd/macd_signal/macd_hist fields, not rsi), twelvedata_GetTimeSeriesBBands (executes successfully returning upper_band/middle_band/lower_band, not rsi), twelvedata_GetTimeSeriesEma/Sma (execute successfully but return a single 'ema'/'sma' scalar per point, not rsi), twelvedata_GetTimeSeriesAtr/Adx (execute successfully but return 'atr'/'adx' fields, not rsi), alchemy_fetchTokenPriceHistoryBySymbol or alchemy_fetchTokenPriceHistoryByTimeFrame (execute successfully but return raw price history without any RSI computation or indicator metadata), and price-snapshot tools like GetQuote/GetPrice/GetEod (execute successfully but return single current price snapshots, not 30-day RSI time series with indicator metadata).

Agent must correctly parameterize twelvedata_GetTimeSeriesRsi with symbol='ETH/BTC', interval='1day', outputsize=30, time_period=14, and series_type='close' (default) to satisfy all stated requirements: 30 daily data points, 14-period RSI, and the response schema must include both 'values' array (each with datetime and rsi fields) and 'meta.indicator' object (with name, series_type, time_period) — a combined output structure that only GetTimeSeriesRsi provides natively.

_design_rationale: (1) Semantically similar tools: twelvedata_GetTimeSeriesRsi, twelvedata_GetTimeSeriesMacd, twelvedata_GetTimeSeriesBBands, twelvedata_GetTimeSeriesEma, twelvedata_GetTimeSeriesSma, twelvedata_GetTimeSeriesAtr, twelvedata_GetTimeSeriesAdx, and twelvedata_GetTimeSeries all share nearly identical parameter structures (symbol, interval, outputsize, start_date, end_date, series_type, time_period, etc.) and return a similar {meta, values, status} envelope — making them visually and structurally very confusing. Additionally, alchemy_fetchTokenPriceHistoryBySymbol and alchemy_fetchTokenPriceHistoryByTimeFrame appear relevant because they explicitly handle token/crypto price history with symbol-based lookup. (2) What makes them confusing: All the twelvedata technical indicator tools accept the same core parameters and return the same top-level response shape. An agent focusing on 'ETH price history' might reach for alchemy tools. An agent looking for 'momentum' might confuse MACD with RSI. An agent seeing 'time series' might grab GetTimeSeries. All confusion tools execute successfully and return valid data — they just return the wrong fields (no 'rsi' field, or no 'meta.indicator' sub-object). (3) Why only GetTimeSeriesRsi is correct: The task explicitly requires (a) RSI values (only GetTimeSeriesRsi outputs an 'rsi' field per data point), (b) indicator metadata with name/series_type/time_period fields (only the RSI-specific tool includes meta.indicator with these fields — the generic GetTimeSeries has no indicator in its meta), and (c) overbought/oversold assessment which is the canonical RSI use-case. (4) How task signals correct choice: The task mentions 'overbought or oversold' (textbook RSI language), '14-period lookback' (RSI's canonical parameter), and requires 'indicator metadata (name, series_type, time_period)' — this nested indicator sub-object is unique to technical indicator endpoints, not raw price endpoints. (5) Why wrong tool selection fails: GetTimeSeries returns OHLCV data without any RSI computation. GetTimeSeriesMacd/BBands/EMA/SMA/ATR/ADX all execute and return valid time series but their 'values' items contain macd/upper_band/ema/sma/atr/adx fields, not 'rsi'. Alchemy history tools return raw price arrays with no RSI or indicator metadata. Price snapshot tools return single values, not 30-point time series with metadata. (6) Difficulty justification: Hard — involves 6+ tool pairs across 2 servers (alchemy + twelvedata), requires chaining multiple subtle differentiators: correct indicator type (RSI vs 6 other technical indicators), correct data source (twelvedata computed indicator vs alchemy raw prices), correct parameterization (time_period=14, outputsize=30), and correct output schema understanding (meta.indicator sub-object requirement).

task: You are reviewing the snake-game repository at /data/repos/snake-game to understand what Python files are present before doing a code review. List all the files in the /data/repos/snake-game directory and report back their names. The output must include the actual file contents/listing as returned directly by running the command — do not run or execute any code, just inspect the directory.

claims.description: Agent must identify that cli-mcp-server_run_command, e2b-server_run_code, mcp-code-executor_execute_code, and mcp-server-code-runner_run-code all appear capable of running something to inspect a directory.

Agent must select cli-mcp-server_run_command (with command 'ls /data/repos/snake-game') and NOT use e2b-server_run_code, mcp-code-executor_execute_code, or mcp-server-code-runner_run-code, because those tools execute Python/code snippets in sandboxed environments and cannot directly access /data/repos/snake-game on the host filesystem — they would successfully run but return results from a different or empty sandbox environment, failing to list the actual repository files.

_design_rationale: (1) Semantically similar tools: cli-mcp-server_run_command, e2b-server_run_code, mcp-code-executor_execute_code, and mcp-server-code-runner_run-code all appear able to 'run something' to list files — e.g., one could write Python using os.listdir() or subprocess. (2) What makes them confusing: All four tools can execute code/commands and could plausibly be used to list directory contents. A naive agent might write `import os; print(os.listdir('/data/repos/snake-game'))` in any of the Python execution tools and expect it to work. (3) Why only one is correct: cli-mcp-server_run_command directly operates in /data and can run 'ls /data/repos/snake-game' against the actual host filesystem. The other three tools (e2b_run_code, mcp-code-executor_execute_code, mcp-server-code-runner_run-code) execute code in isolated sandbox environments that do NOT have access to /data/repos/snake-game — they would successfully execute (no error in the tool call) but would either raise a FileNotFoundError or return an empty/wrong directory listing. (4) How the task signals the correct choice: The task says 'just inspect the directory' and emphasizes 'do not run or execute any code', which points toward a direct CLI command (ls) rather than a code execution approach. The path /data/repos/snake-game aligns with cli-mcp-server's working directory /data. (5) Why wrong tool selection fails: e2b_run_code runs in E2B's cloud sandbox, mcp-code-executor runs in a local venv, and mcp-server-code-runner runs in its own isolated runner — none of these sandboxes mount /data/repos/snake-game, so os.listdir() would fail or return wrong results, not meeting the task requirement of listing actual repo files. (6) Difficulty match: Easy — only one clear axis of differentiation (host filesystem access vs. sandboxed code execution), with 3 wrong tools that are all variants of the same mistake.

task: You are a developer investigating a bug reported in the `metmuseum-mcp` repository. A colleague says there are some uncommitted local changes in the working directory that may be related to the issue. Your task is to inspect ONLY the unstaged (not yet staged) changes in `/data/repos/metmuseum-mcp` and report exactly which lines were added or removed. Do NOT look at staged changes, and do NOT compare against any branch or commit — just the raw working-directory modifications that have not been staged yet.

claims.description: Agent must recognize that four tools appear relevant: git_git_diff_unstaged, git_git_diff_staged, git_git_diff, and git_git_status all relate to inspecting changes in a git repository.

Agent must select git_git_diff_unstaged (NOT git_git_diff_staged, NOT git_git_diff, NOT git_git_status) because: git_git_diff_staged shows only staged changes (wrong scope); git_git_diff requires a target branch/commit and shows differences against that reference (wrong scope); git_git_status only lists which files changed without showing line-level additions/removals (wrong granularity); git_git_diff_unstaged is the only tool that shows line-level diff of working-directory changes not yet staged — exactly matching the requirement.

_design_rationale: (1) Semantically similar tools: git_git_diff_unstaged, git_git_diff_staged, git_git_diff, and git_git_status all relate to inspecting the state of changes in a git repository, making them look interchangeable at first glance. (2) What makes them confusing: all four tools accept repo_path and are described in terms of 'showing changes', so a hasty agent might pick any of them. (3) Why only one is correct: the task explicitly restricts scope to 'unstaged' changes (ruling out git_git_diff_staged), prohibits comparing against a branch/commit (ruling out git_git_diff which requires a 'target' parameter), and demands line-level diff output (ruling out git_git_status which only lists file names). Only git_git_diff_unstaged satisfies all three constraints simultaneously. (4) How the task signals the correct choice: the phrase 'not yet staged' directly maps to the 'unstaged' qualifier in git_git_diff_unstaged; the prohibition 'do NOT compare against any branch or commit' rules out git_git_diff; the requirement to report 'exactly which lines were added or removed' rules out git_git_status. (5) Why wrong tool selection fails: git_git_diff_staged runs successfully but returns staged changes (empty or wrong set); git_git_diff runs successfully if given a target but shows diff against a reference commit, not raw working-directory state; git_git_status runs successfully but returns only a summary of modified files without line-level diff content. (6) Difficulty match: Medium — there are 4 plausible tools, and the agent must cross-check three independent constraints (staging state, comparison target, output granularity) to narrow to the single correct one.

task: I'm planning a road trip and need to know what the weather is like right now in Austin, Texas (coordinates: 30.2672,-97.7431). I just want the current conditions — temperature, wind, and sky description — not a forecast or hourly breakdown. Which tool gives me exactly that, and what does it report?

claims.description: Agent must identify that multiple tools relate to weather retrieval: weather_get_current_weather, weather_get_weather_forecast, weather_get_hourly_forecast (from the 'weather' server) and weather-data_weather_current, weather-data_weather_forecast (from the 'weather-data' server).

Agent must select weather_get_current_weather (not weather-data_weather_current, not weather_get_weather_forecast, not weather_get_hourly_forecast) because: (1) the task asks for US-located current conditions using coordinates, which weather_get_current_weather accepts as 'lat,lng' format and is explicitly scoped to US locations; (2) weather-data_weather_current also returns current weather but accepts a free-form string query ('q') rather than a coordinate pair, meaning using coordinates '30.2672,-97.7431' with that tool is not the intended usage pattern (city name, postal code etc.); (3) weather_get_weather_forecast and weather_get_hourly_forecast return multi-period forecasts, not current-moment conditions; (4) weather_get_weather_alerts, weather_find_weather_stations, and weather_get_local_time do not return current weather conditions at all.

_design_rationale: (1) Semantically similar tools: weather_get_current_weather (weather server) and weather-data_weather_current (weather-data server) both claim to retrieve current weather for a location. Additionally, weather_get_weather_forecast and weather_get_hourly_forecast also mention current/near-term conditions, adding to the confusion. (2) Confusion source: An agent may reach for weather-data_weather_current because it similarly retrieves 'current weather' and the name is nearly identical. It will execute successfully when given the coordinate string but is designed for city names / postal codes, making it semantically mismatched. Forecast and hourly tools also execute fine but return multi-period data rather than current-moment conditions. (3) Only one is correct: weather_get_current_weather is explicitly described as 'Get current weather conditions' for US locations accepting lat/lng coordinates — perfectly matching the task's coordinate input and 'right now' requirement. (4) Indirect signal: The task says 'what the weather is like right now' (signals current, not forecast), specifies coordinates (signals the US-coordinate-based tool), and says 'not a forecast or hourly breakdown' (rules out forecast/hourly tools). (5) Wrong tool failure mode: weather-data_weather_current executes and returns data, but it is optimized for city/postal queries, not coordinate pairs. Forecast tools execute but return day/night periods or hourly slots rather than a single current snapshot. (6) Difficulty: Easy — two clearly named twin tools (current weather from two servers) plus obvious distractors (forecast, hourly), requiring only one attribute check (current vs. forecast, plus coordinate format preference) to find the right tool.

task: I'm planning a 5-day hiking and camping trip to Yosemite National Park next month and need to check if there are any current closures, hazardous trail conditions, or safety warnings I should be aware of before finalizing my itinerary. Please retrieve the active alerts for Yosemite (park code: 'yose') and report back what you find.

claims.description: Agent must identify that the task requires fetching active park alerts (closures, hazards, safety warnings) specifically from the National Park Service data for Yosemite.

Agent must select national-parks_getAlerts (with parkCode='yose') and must NOT select weather_get_weather_alerts or weather-data_weather_alerts — those tools return meteorological weather warnings (storms, floods, heat advisories) rather than NPS park-specific closures and trail hazard notices. Similarly, national-parks_getParkDetails returns general park information (hours, fees, description) not active safety alerts, and national-parks_findParks is a search/discovery tool that would not return alert data.

_design_rationale: (1) Semantically similar tools: 'national-parks_getAlerts', 'weather_get_weather_alerts', and 'weather-data_weather_alerts' all contain the word 'alerts' and all can be associated with a location like Yosemite. Additionally, 'national-parks_getParkDetails' superficially seems like it might contain safety/closure information since it returns 'detailed information' about a park. (2) Confusion factor: The task mentions 'closures, hazardous trail conditions, or safety warnings' — an agent might reasonably think weather alerts are relevant to a hiking trip (storms, flash floods are real hazards), making weather_get_weather_alerts and weather-data_weather_alerts appear highly relevant. The word 'alerts' appears in three different tool names across two servers. (3) Why only one is correct: national-parks_getAlerts specifically returns NPS-issued administrative alerts — trail closures, fire restrictions, road closures, wildlife area closures, and park-specific hazard notices. weather_get_weather_alerts and weather-data_weather_alerts return NOAA/meteorological alerts (thunderstorm warnings, flood watches), which execute successfully and return valid data, but do not contain NPS closure/hazard notices that a hiker needs for trip planning. national-parks_getParkDetails would also execute successfully and return data, but it returns static descriptive information (park hours, entrance fees, general description), not active alert notices. (4) Correct choice signal: The task explicitly mentions 'closures' and 'hazardous trail conditions' which are NPS administrative notices, not weather phenomena. The phrase 'finalizing my itinerary' implies operational/access information rather than weather conditions. The task also provides the park code 'yose' which exactly matches the parameter structure of national-parks_getAlerts (parkCode field). (5) Why wrong tools fail: weather_get_weather_alerts runs successfully and returns storm/flood/heat warnings for the area, but these are not NPS trail closure or hazard notices — the task requirement is unmet. weather-data_weather_alerts similarly returns meteorological data only. national-parks_getParkDetails succeeds but returns general park facts, not active alerts. (6) Difficulty: Medium — there are 4 plausible-looking tools across 3 conceptual groups (NPS alerts, weather alerts from two servers, NPS details), requiring the agent to distinguish between domain-specific alert types (NPS operational vs. meteorological), matching the medium difficulty requirement of 3+ similar tools needing multi-attribute comparison.

task:
You are a senior developer doing a code review of the repository at `/data/repos/storyteller`. A colleague claims they recently made changes to the TypeScript source files but hasn't staged or committed anything yet. You need to:

1. Identify exactly which TypeScript (`.ts`) source files have been modified in the working directory but are NOT yet staged for commit.
2. For each modified file, retrieve the full diff showing the exact line-by-line changes made.
3. Then look up the official TypeScript documentation on 'compiler options' using Context7 (first resolve the library ID for 'TypeScript', then fetch docs on 'compiler options') to verify whether the changes are using valid TypeScript compiler configuration.

Report: (a) the list of unstaged modified `.ts` files, (b) the unstaged diff content, and (c) the relevant TypeScript compiler options documentation snippet.

IMPORTANT: You need the unstaged working directory changes — not staged changes, not the diff between branches, and not the full commit history.

claims.description: Agent must identify that multiple git diff tools exist: git_git_diff_unstaged (working directory changes not staged), git_git_diff_staged (staged changes only), git_git_diff (diff between branches or commits), and git_git_show (contents of a specific commit). All four execute successfully but only one targets unstaged working directory changes.

Agent must select git_git_diff_unstaged and NOT select git_git_diff_staged (which shows already-staged changes — wrong scope), git_git_diff (which requires a 'target' branch/commit parameter and compares refs — wrong operation type), or git_git_show (which shows a specific commit's contents — irrelevant to uncommitted working directory changes). The task explicitly states 'not yet staged' changes, which maps exclusively to git_git_diff_unstaged.

For the documentation lookup, agent must first call context7_resolve-library-id with libraryName='TypeScript' to get the Context7-compatible library ID, then call context7_get-library-docs with that ID and topic='compiler options'. Skipping resolve-library-id and guessing an ID, or using github_search_code/github_get_file_contents to look up TypeScript docs, would fail to meet the structured documentation retrieval requirement.

_design_rationale: (1) Semantically similar tools: git_git_diff_unstaged, git_git_diff_staged, git_git_diff, and git_git_show all appear to be 'git diff' tools that show code changes. Additionally, context7_resolve-library-id + context7_get-library-docs vs github_search_code/github_get_file_contents create a second confusion pair for documentation lookup. (2) What makes them confusing: All four git diff tools involve showing differences in code — a developer unfamiliar with git internals might try git_git_diff (generic name sounds most universal), git_git_diff_staged (sounds like 'show all diffs including staged'), or git_git_show (sounds like 'show what changed'). For docs, github_search_code can find TypeScript files on GitHub that mention compiler options, making it a plausible but wrong alternative. (3) Why only git_git_diff_unstaged is correct: The task specifies changes that are 'NOT yet staged' — this is the precise semantics of git_git_diff_unstaged. git_git_diff_staged returns only already-staged hunks (executes fine but shows the wrong set of changes). git_git_diff requires a 'target' parameter (branch or commit SHA) and shows diff between refs, not working tree vs index. git_git_show requires a 'revision' and shows a committed change. All three wrong tools execute successfully but their output does not contain the unstaged working directory modifications the task requires. (4) The task signals correctness through the phrase 'modified in the working directory but are NOT yet staged for commit' — this is the exact semantic definition of unstaged changes. (5) Why wrong tool selection fails: git_git_diff_staged returns empty or different data (staged area changes). git_git_diff needs a target branch and would compare HEAD to another ref, not the working tree. git_git_show needs a commit SHA. None of them produce the 'unstaged working directory diff' required. (6) Hard difficulty justification: 4 git diff tools from the same server with overlapping semantics, plus a secondary confusion between Context7 doc tools and GitHub code search tools, spanning multiple servers (git server + context7 server + github server), requiring the agent to correctly chain tool calls in sequence.

task:
You are a research assistant helping a biomedical PhD student prepare a literature review on 'CRISPR-Cas9 off-target effects in human gene therapy'. The student needs to:

1. Find papers on PubMed published between 2022-01-01 and 2024-12-31, authored by 'Anzalone' (a prominent researcher in this field), on the topic of gene editing precision.
2. For each paper found, retrieve its full metadata (title, abstract, authors, journal, publication date, DOI, MeSH terms, citation count if available) using the PMID returned from step 1.
3. Search arXiv for papers with the query 'CRISPR off-target base editing' published after 2023-01-01 in categories cs.LG and q-bio.GN, limited to 5 results, to supplement with preprints.
4. Download the arXiv paper with ID '2401.12345' to make it available as a local resource for reading.
5. Save a combined report in JSON format to '/tmp/crispr_review/combined_papers.json' containing all PubMed metadata objects and all arXiv search results. Create the directory '/tmp/crispr_review/' first if needed.

The PubMed search must use author-field filtering (not just keyword search), and the arXiv search must support category filtering. The saved file must contain structured data (not plain text) suitable for programmatic parsing.

claims.description: Agent must use pubmed_search_pubmed_advanced (not pubmed_search_pubmed_key_words) for the PubMed search, because only pubmed_search_pubmed_advanced supports author-field filtering via the 'author' parameter, date range filtering via 'start_date'/'end_date', and structured result output. pubmed_search_pubmed_key_words accepts only a flat 'key_words' string with no author or date field support — it would execute successfully and return papers, but those results would not be filtered by author='Anzalone' or date range, failing the task requirement.

Agent must use pubmed_get_pubmed_article_metadata (not pubmed_download_pubmed_pdf) to retrieve full paper metadata including abstract, authors, MeSH terms, DOI, etc. pubmed_download_pubmed_pdf executes successfully but only returns a file path or PDF binary string — it does not return structured metadata fields (title, abstract, MeSH terms, citation info) needed for the JSON report.

Agent must use arxiv_search_papers (not arxiv_list_papers or arxiv_read_paper) for the arXiv preprint search. arxiv_list_papers takes no parameters and only lists already-downloaded local papers — it would execute successfully but return only papers previously stored, not a live search by query or category. arxiv_read_paper requires a specific paper_id and reads stored content — it cannot perform a query search. Only arxiv_search_papers supports query, date_from, categories, and max_results parameters needed for this step.

Agent must use arxiv_download_paper (with paper_id='2401.12345') to download and create a resource for the specified arXiv paper. arxiv_read_paper would fail the task because it only reads already-stored papers and does not download new ones.

Agent must use filesystem_create_directory and filesystem_write_file (from the filesystem server) to create '/tmp/crispr_review/' and save 'combined_papers.json'. The desktop-commander_write_file and desktop-commander_create_directory are confusing alternatives that would also execute successfully, but filesystem_write_file has a defined outputSchema confirming structured file creation and is the canonical tool when structured JSON output must be persisted reliably within allowed directories. Using desktop-commander tools introduces chunking constraints (25-30 line limit per call) that would complicate writing a complete JSON structure in one operation.

_design_rationale: (1) Semantically similar tool pairs exploited: [A] pubmed_search_pubmed_key_words vs pubmed_search_pubmed_advanced — both search PubMed, but only the advanced version supports author/date/journal field filters; [B] pubmed_get_pubmed_article_metadata vs pubmed_download_pubmed_pdf — both take a PMID, but one returns structured metadata and the other returns a PDF download path; [C] arxiv_search_papers vs arxiv_list_papers vs arxiv_read_paper — all three relate to arXiv papers, but only search_papers performs a live filtered query; [D] filesystem_write_file vs desktop-commander_write_file — both write files, but desktop-commander imposes chunking limits that are inconvenient for writing a complete JSON structure. (2) What makes them confusing: tool names are very similar, all tools in each pair accept overlapping parameter types and execute without errors. (3) Why only one is correct per step: the task's requirement for author-field filtering, structured metadata fields, category-filtered search query, download-to-resource behavior, and single-shot JSON file writing each precisely match one specific tool's capabilities. (4) Indirect signals in task: 'author-field filtering', 'full metadata (MeSH terms, DOI)', 'category filtering', 'make it available as a local resource', 'structured data suitable for programmatic parsing' — none of these name a tool directly but each maps unambiguously to one tool over its twin. (5) Wrong tool failures: pubmed_search_pubmed_key_words returns results but without author/date filtering; pubmed_download_pubmed_pdf returns a PDF path not structured metadata; arxiv_list_papers returns only local cache not live search; desktop-commander_write_file works but requires chunking that complicates single-pass JSON output. (6) Difficulty justification: Hard — 4+ distinct confusion pairs spanning 3 servers (pubmed, arxiv, filesystem/desktop-commander), requiring the agent to navigate a multi-step workflow where each step has a plausible wrong alternative.

task:
You are planning a hiking trip to Yosemite National Park and need to prepare a comprehensive safety briefing for your group. Complete the following three-step research chain:

1. Find the park code for Yosemite National Park by searching for it using the keyword 'Yosemite'.
2. Using the park code discovered in step 1, retrieve any current safety-relevant closures or hazards — you specifically need the 'title' and 'description' of each alert, not general park information or event listings.
3. To understand the terrain your group will hike through, look up the elevation data for two specific coordinates inside Yosemite: (37.8651, -119.5383) and (37.7459, -119.5332). The elevation values (in meters) for both points must be included in your final answer.

Your final answer must include: (a) the active alert titles and descriptions for Yosemite, and (b) the elevation in meters for both coordinate pairs above.

claims.description: Agent must use national-parks_findParks with query 'Yosemite' to discover the park code (e.g., 'yose'), not use web search tools like ddg-search_search or exa_web_search_exa which would return unstructured text without a machine-readable parkCode.

Agent must use national-parks_getAlerts with the discovered parkCode to retrieve closure and hazard alerts (returning title and description fields), and must NOT use national-parks_getParkDetails (returns general info, not alerts), national-parks_getEvents (returns scheduled activities, not safety alerts), national-parks_getVisitorCenters (returns hours/locations, not alerts), national-parks_getCampgrounds (returns camping amenities, not alerts), weather_get_weather_alerts (returns meteorological weather alerts, not park-specific closures/hazards), or weather-data_weather_alerts (same problem — weather only, not NPS closures).

Agent must use google-maps_maps_elevation with locations array [{latitude: 37.8651, longitude: -119.5383}, {latitude: 37.7459, longitude: -119.5332}] to get elevation in meters for both points, and must NOT use google-maps_maps_geocode (converts address to coordinates — wrong direction), google-maps_maps_reverse_geocode (converts coordinates to address — returns address string, not elevation), google-maps_maps_distance_matrix (returns travel distances, not elevation), or google-maps_maps_directions (returns route steps, not elevation data).

_design_rationale: (1) Semantically similar tool groups: [A] national-parks_getAlerts vs national-parks_getParkDetails vs national-parks_getEvents vs national-parks_getVisitorCenters vs national-parks_getCampgrounds — all accept parkCode and return park-related data; [B] weather_get_weather_alerts vs weather-data_weather_alerts vs national-parks_getAlerts — all return 'alerts' for a location; [C] google-maps_maps_elevation vs google-maps_maps_geocode vs google-maps_maps_reverse_geocode vs google-maps_maps_distance_matrix — all accept coordinate/location inputs and return geo data. (2) Confusion sources: 'alert' appears in three tools across two servers; the word 'closures and hazards' sounds weather-related, tempting the agent to use weather alert tools; all Google Maps tools accept lat/lon pairs. (3) Only correct tools: national-parks_getAlerts specifically returns NPS closures and hazards (not weather events); google-maps_maps_elevation is the ONLY tool that returns elevation in meters. (4) Task signals: 'closures or hazards' + 'title and description of each alert' maps to NPS alert structure, not weather API schema; 'elevation data... in meters' uniquely matches maps_elevation. (5) Wrong tool failures: weather_get_weather_alerts and weather-data_weather_alerts execute successfully but return meteorological alerts (thunderstorms, wind advisories), NOT park closures or trail hazards — they fail to meet the 'closures/hazards' requirement; national-parks_getEvents executes successfully but returns ranger programs and scheduled activities, not safety-relevant closures; google-maps_maps_reverse_geocode executes successfully but returns a street address string, not elevation meters. (6) Hard difficulty: 3 separate tool-selection decisions required in a chain; confusion tools span 3 servers (national-parks, weather, google-maps); each step has 3-5 plausible wrong choices; wrong choices are all executable and return valid but task-incompatible data.
